summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

The line of best fit predicts y = 25, but the actual value is 28. What is the residual?

Residual = actual - predicted = 28 - 25 = 3. A positive residual means the actual value is above the predicted value.

A data set has residuals: -2, 1, -1, 3, -1. What is the sum of these residuals?

Sum = -2 + 1 + (-1) + 3 + (-1) = 0. For a least-squares regression line, the sum of the residuals is always approximately zero.

What is a residual?

A residual is the difference between an actual observed value and the corresponding predicted value from the regression model, calculated as residual = actual - predicted.

What is a residual plot?

A residual plot is a graph where the residuals are plotted on the y-axis against either the independent variable or predicted values on the x-axis, used to assess how well a model fits the data.

What is the formula for calculating a residual?

The residual is calculated as the actual observed value minus the predicted value from the model: residual = actual - predicted.

What is the purpose of creating a residual plot?

The primary purpose of a residual plot is to evaluate whether a linear model is a good fit for the data by checking whether the residuals show random scatter or a systematic pattern.

An online retailer models shipping time (days, y) based on distance (hundreds of miles, x) with y = 0.3x + 1. For orders shipped 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 hundred miles, actual delivery times were 2.5, 4.2, 5.3, 7.1, and 8.6 days. What is the largest absolute residual?

Predicted: 0.3(5)+1=2.5, 0.3(10)+1=4, 0.3(15)+1=5.5, 0.3(20)+1=7, 0.3(25)+1=8.5. Residuals: 2.5-2.5=0, 4.2-4=0.2, 5.3-5.5=-0.2, 7.1-7=0.1, 8.6-8.5=0.1. The largest absolute residual is 0.2.

A city planner models the relationship between distance from downtown (miles, x) and average rent (hundreds of dollars, y) using a least-squares line. She gets residuals of 1.2, -0.8, 0.3, -1.5, and 0.8 for five data points. What is the sum of these residuals, and is this consistent with a properly fitted least-squares line?

Sum = 1.2 + (-0.8) + 0.3 + (-1.5) + 0.8 = 0. For a least-squares regression line, the sum of all residuals is always zero, so this is consistent with a properly fitted model.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A veterinarian models pet weight (lbs, x) and medication dosage (mg, y) using y = 0.5x + 2. For five pets weighing 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 lbs, the actual dosages are 8, 11, 18, 22, 28. What are the residuals?

Predicted: 0.5(10)+2=7, 0.5(20)+2=12, 0.5(30)+2=17, 0.5(40)+2=22, 0.5(50)+2=27. Residuals: 8-7=1, 11-12=-1, 18-17=1, 22-22=0, 28-27=1.

Example: A bakery models daily donut sales (y) based on the number of nearby events (x) with y = 15x + 80. On days with 2, 4, 6, and 8 events, actual sales were 115, 138, 175, and 198. Calculate the residuals and determine if the linear model appears appropriate.

Predicted: 15(2)+80=110, 15(4)+80=140, 15(6)+80=170, 15(8)+80=200. Residuals: 115-110=5, 138-140=-2, 175-170=5, 198-200=-2. The alternating pattern with small values suggests the linear model is reasonable.

Example: A gym tracks monthly membership length (months, x) and average visits per month (y). The line of best fit is y = -0.5x + 20. After 6 months, members average 18 visits; after 12 months, 15 visits; after 18 months, 8 visits; after 24 months, 5 visits. The residual plot shows increasing negative residuals. What does this pattern suggest?

Predicted: 17, 14, 11, 8. Residuals: 18-17=1, 15-14=1, 8-11=-3, 5-8=-3. The residuals shift from positive to increasingly negative, suggesting visits drop off faster than the linear model predicts, and a non-linear model might be better.

Example: A car insurance company uses the model y = 50x + 200 to estimate annual claims (y, in dollars) based on driver age minus 16 (x, years of driving). For drivers with 2, 5, 10, 15, and 20 years of experience, actual claims are $310, $460, $680, $940, and $1220. Calculate the residuals and evaluate the model.

Predicted: 50(2)+200=300, 50(5)+200=450, 50(10)+200=700, 50(15)+200=950, 50(20)+200=1200. Residuals: 310-300=10, 460-450=10, 680-700=-20, 940-950=-10, 1220-1200=20. The residuals scatter randomly, suggesting the linear model is a reasonable fit.

Example: A restaurant chain models daily revenue (y, in hundreds of dollars) using y = 2x + 10, where x is the number of Yelp stars (1-5). Locations with 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 stars have revenues of $1250, $1380, $1620, $1800, and $2050. After plotting residuals, the manager sees residuals of 0.5, -0.2, 0.2, -0.2, 0.5. Are there any concerns?

The residuals are very small (all less than 1 in magnitude on a scale of hundreds of dollars) and show no strong systematic pattern. This indicates the linear model fits the data well with only minor deviations.

Example: A city planner models the relationship between distance from downtown (miles, x) and average rent (hundreds of dollars, y) using a least-squares line. She gets residuals of 1.2, -0.8, 0.3, -1.5, and 0.8 for five data points. What is the sum of these residuals, and is this consistent with a properly fitted least-squares line?

Sum = 1.2 + (-0.8) + 0.3 + (-1.5) + 0.8 = 0. For a least-squares regression line, the sum of all residuals is always zero, so this is consistent with a properly fitted model.
